title: Topping up loft insulation with blown loose-fill mineral wool
subtitle: Using a hired blowing machine to reach 270 mm depth across an accessible loft
excerpt: A loft with 100 mm of old glass wool has a U-value of around 0.45 W/m2K. Topping it up to 270 mm with blown loose-fill mineral wool reduces that to around 0.16 W/m2K and can save 100 to 150 kWh per year in a typical three-bedroom semi. The work takes one day with a hired machine and a helper feeding the hopper at ground level.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Part L1B (2022) for the 0.16 W/m2K loft target at 270 mm. Energy Saving Trust loft insulation guidance 2024. CIBSE Guide A for U-value derivation at standard joist depths.

glossaryTerms.definition: Insulation made from spun glass or rock fibres. Loose-fill mineral wool is blown in as a cloud of fibre that settles to fill irregular spaces. Dense-pack versions resist compaction and air movement.
The final depth of loose-fill insulation after it has compacted slightly under gravity. Installers overfill by 15 to 25 percent to reach the target settled depth.
The rate of heat transfer through a building element in W/m2K. Lower is better. A 270 mm mineral wool loft at U 0.16 loses heat roughly three times more slowly than 100 mm at U 0.45.

content.text: Loose-fill
mineral wool

blown over the loft floor is one of the cheapest insulation upgrades available. A hired machine forces the loose fibre through a flexible hose into the loft while a helper at ground level feeds sacks into the hopper. On a typical 50 m2 semi, the whole job from unloading to clearing up takes around six hours. The material cost is low because it goes on top of whatever is already there, no removal required.

Households receiving qualifying benefits can get loft insulation fitted free under the government ECO4 scheme. Check gov.uk before hiring a machine: if you qualify, a registered installer does the work at no cost. This tutorial covers the DIY approach for those who do not qualify or prefer to do it themselves.
What you need

Blown loose-fill mineral wool sacks, 15 kg each. Hire depots will estimate bag count from your floor area; allow one 15 kg sack per 2 m2 to reach 270 mm settled depth over 100 mm existing, plus 10 percent for peaks and joists.
Blowing machine, hired by the day. The machine is often free when you buy a minimum number of sacks from the same supplier.
Timber depth markers, 300 mm length cut from softwood offcuts, to place across the loft floor at 1 m centres as a visual guide to the target depth.
Polythene sheeting or cardboard dams to block the eaves. These stop insulation from spilling into the ventilation gap at the top of the outer wall.

Blown mineral wool generates significant dust. Wear an FFP2 mask and eye protection throughout. Ensure the loft hatch can be closed while the machine runs to prevent dust spreading into the house. Work in the loft with a headtorch and a solid board across the joists, never stepping directly on the ceiling.
Preparation
Clear any stored items from the loft floor. Sweep loose debris and old insulation fragments away from the eaves with a vacuum if necessary so the depth markers read accurately.
Insulate the loft hatch lid. A 100 mm slab of rigid foam board fixed to the hatch lid with adhesive closes what is otherwise a thermal hole in the middle of the new insulation layer.

Fit eaves dams at the top of each external wall. These stop the blown material from blocking the 25 mm ventilation gap between the eaves and the top of the outer wall. Use cut polythene or shaped foam blocks wedged between the top of the wall and the underside of the rafter.
Push the timber depth markers into the existing insulation at 1 m centres across the loft floor. Mark the 270 mm target on each one with a permanent marker before going up.
Blowing the insulation

Thread the blower hose through the loft hatch. The person in the loft directs the nozzle while the helper at ground level feeds sacks into the machine hopper. Communicate via a two-call signal (one shout for stop, two for go) to avoid overloading the hose.
Work from the far end of the loft toward the hatch. Keep the nozzle 150 to 200 mm above the target level to allow the blown fibre to settle and fill gaps. The final
settled depth
will be 15 to 25 mm less than the blown depth, so overshoot the depth markers slightly.
Sweep the hose back and forth in overlapping arcs to fill the full width between and over the joists. Do not leave the hose stationary or the fibre will pile into a cone.
When complete, measure the final settled depth at three points across the loft. The average should meet the target depth for the
U-value
declared on the product bag. For mineral wool rated at 0.044 W/mK, 270 mm of settled material gives U 0.16 W/m2K.
What done well looks like

The depth markers are fully submerged across the whole loft floor including the perimeter bays. The eaves dams are intact and the 25 mm ventilation void above each external wall is clear. The loft hatch lid has its own insulation layer. No loose fibre has entered the house.
What done badly looks like

The perimeter bays near the eaves are thin because the operator did not push the hose far enough into the corner. The eaves dam slipped during blowing and insulation has filled the ventilation void, raising the risk of roof deck condensation. The loft hatch is uninsulated, creating a 0.6 m2 cold patch in the middle of an otherwise well-insulated floor.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Dust coming into the house during blowing
attrs.cause: Loft hatch was left open or poorly sealed while the machine ran
attrs.fix: Stop the machine, seal the hatch with draught-excluding tape before restarting, and direct any overhead lights in the room below away from the hatch to reduce convective draw.

attrs.cause: Damp sacks, or sacks loaded too fast without breaking up clumps first
attrs.fix: Tear the sack open and break up any compressed clumps before tipping into the hopper. If sacks are damp from storage, allow them to dry for 30 minutes in a warm room before use.

attrs.symptom: Depth is uneven with thin patches over the central joists
attrs.cause: The joists channelled the flow so fibre piled on top of the joists rather than settling evenly
attrs.fix: Use a garden rake (or a long timber offcut) to redistribute the fibre after blowing, drawing it from the high points over the joists into the low bays between them.
